title: When to Use Mixup/CutMix
concept: Mixup and CutMix are highly effective for image classification tasks. However, they are less suited for tasks requiring precise pixel coordinates, like object detection and segmentation, where blended boundaries can confuse localization.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does CutMix force a model to distribute its attention across the entire image?

options.text: Because the pasted patch and background belong to different classes, so every region matters for the soft label
Because it doubles the resolution of the image
Because it removes the loss function entirely

exercises.explain: Yes! Every region contributes to the soft label, forcing distributed attention.

cards.title: Great for Classification
cards.body: Mixup and CutMix are standard defaults in modern image classification pipelines (like training ResNets on ImageNet). They consistently boost accuracy by 1-2%. But if your model needs to draw a precise bounding box around an object, blending images presents a serious challenge.

cards.title: Localization Limitations
cards.body: While excellent for global classification, standard Mixup and CutMix are less suited for localization tasks like object detection or semantic segmentation. Blending pixel boundaries or cutting out parts of boxes makes it difficult for the network to learn precise edges.

cards.title: The archer's target
cards.body: If you are training to hit a bullseye, you need a clear, solid target. Mixup is like making the target semi-transparent and shifting it around — you can't tell where the exact center is anymore. For precise targeting, you need clear boundaries, not blends.

cards.title: Object Detection
cards.body: In object detection, a model must predict a bounding box [X, Y, Width, Height]. If we apply Mixup, the car is blended with a kitchen background. Drawing a box around a semi-transparent car is mathematically messy and degrades box accuracy.

cards.title: Not a universal cure
cards.body: Don't assume image mixing should be turned on for every computer vision model you build. It is a specialized tool for regularization. If your dataset is huge and overfitting isn't an issue, mixing might actually slow down training convergence without adding value.

cards.title: When to Mix vs. Not
cards.body: Choosing when to apply Mixup or CutMix:

left.text:
Image Classification
Small datasets prone to overfitting
Fine-grained classification tasks

right.text:
Object Detection (bounding boxes)
Semantic Segmentation (pixel masks)
Huge datasets with low overfitting risk

exercises.prompt: For which of these tasks is standard Mixup/CutMix MOST suitable?

options.text: Classifying whether an image contains a cat, dog, or bird
Drawing bounding boxes around pedestrians in self-driving cars
Coloring in every individual pixel of a road in a satellite image
Measuring the exact height of a container box in a warehouse

exercises.explain: Correct! Image classification is the primary target for these mixing augmentations.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If you have a massive dataset and your model is already underfitting, adding aggressive Mixup will help speed up training.
exercises.explain: False. Mixup acts as a regularizer, which can make training harder. If the model is underfitting, adding regularization will make it worse.

exercises.sentence: Standard Mixup is rarely used in object detection because blending boundaries confuses target ___ coordinates.
exercises.explain: Blended pixel edges confuse the precise coordinates needed for bounding box prediction.

exercises.prompt: Why do Mixup and CutMix act as regularizers?

options.text: They smooth the decision boundary by preventing the model from predicting extreme confidences
They reduce the number of channels in the convolutional layer
They force the weights of the classification head to remain constant

exercises.explain: Correct! Soft targets smooth decision boundaries, regularizing the training.

exercises.prompt: A teammate suggests using CutMix for a medical segmentation model that labels tumor pixels. What is the main risk?

options.text: Pasting a sharp rectangular patch of tumor onto a healthy organ creates unrealistic, artificial boundary edges that don't match how tumors grow in real tissues
CutMix will automatically change the tumor cells to healthy cells, causing severe label corruption
There is no risk; CutMix is the default standard for all medical imaging models
CutMix is unsafe because medical images cannot be loaded into a computer pipeline

exercises.explain: Exactly! The artificial collage edges can confuse segmentation models. Next, let's explore preprocessing pipelines and how to structure all these steps!
